ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a distinct architectural idea—doubly-recurrent neural network cells that maintain separate ancestral (depth) and fraternal (width/sibling) recurrences and combine them to predict node labels (Eqs.1–3)—plus an explicit, decoupled topological prediction mechanism (pai, pfi in Eqs.5–6) instead of padding tokens. The authors clearly contrast this design with prior top-down Tree-LSTM and SEQ2TREE approaches (Zhang et al. 2016; Dong & Lapata 2016) and emphasize end-to-end training and simultaneous handling of depth/width signals. While the core elements build on existing tree/LSTM ideas, the simultaneous dual-recurrence and explicit topology prediction constitute a notable, non-trivial extension rather than a trivial combination of prior techniques.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states its main claims (DRNN with separate ancestral/fraternal recurrences and explicit topology prediction) clearly (Sections 1–3) and supports them with experiments on synthetic tree recovery and IFTTT program generation (Section 4) showing improved F1 over relevant baselines. The mathematical description (Eqs.1–7) is coherent and the training/backpropagation procedure (Section 3.2 and Appendix B.1) is plausible for the proposed architecture. Weaknesses are experimental: no statistical significance tests, limited-scale MT experiments, and some training choices (teacher forcing, averaging gradients from multiple children) are acknowledged but not fully justified or ablated, and the sibling-order assumption is not deeply discussed. Overall the technical claims are supported but with minor methodological gaps that do not negate the main conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Discussion), and the main contributions are called out explicitly in the Introduction with a clear bullet list. The core notation for the model is introduced systematically in Section 3 (definitions of T, ga/gf, Eqs. (1)–(4)) and the topological prediction mechanism is described concretely (Eqs. (5)–(7)), which makes the method easy to follow. Presentation issues are minor: there are small notational/formatting inconsistencies (e.g. occasional spacing/notation like h (pred)i vs h(pred)i and xp(i)/xs(j) usage), a few grammatical slips (e.g. “These ideas has been adopted”), and some duplicated/garbled lines in the appendix, but these do not materially impede understanding.
